subtitle: The bulky calf muscle — prone to strains

strava_risk_triggers.risk_note: Gastrocnemius strains common when returning to running after rest periods

mild.description: Tightness or ache in the upper calf, eases after warm-up
mild.can_run_note: Yes — warm up thoroughly and avoid speed work
why: Gastrocnemius tightness from sustained plantar flexion effort
relief: Standing calf stretch: wall lean with straight knee 60s each side
Easy walking or cycling to maintain blood flow
prevention: Eccentric calf raises on a step 3 × 15 daily
Dynamic calf warm-up before all runs

moderate.description: Sharp pulling or tightening in upper calf mid-run
moderate.can_run_note: Easy pace only — stop immediately if sharp pain returns
why: Partial gastrocnemius strain — micro-tearing in the muscle belly
Tennis leg: medial gastrocnemius tear at the musculotendinous junction
relief: RICE for 48 hours if acute strain
Heel raise insole to reduce calf loading temporarily
prevention: Stay well hydrated — dehydration predisposes to calf strains
Never increase pace without a proper warm-up

severe.description: Acute pop in the calf, immediate severe pain, unable to run
severe.can_run_note: Do not run — possible grade 2 or 3 tear
why: Grade 2 or 3 gastrocnemius tear — common in athletes over 30
Tennis leg: complete medial head tear
DVT must be ruled out if significant calf swelling develops
relief: Ice and compression immediately — elevate leg
See GP urgently if significant swelling — rule out DVT
prevention: 6–12 week return-to-run programme depending on grade
Progressive calf loading before return to speed work

see_physio_if: A pop or snap was felt at the time of injury
Significant bruising tracking down to the ankle
Calf swelling that does not reduce with elevation — rule out DVT
